rationale: Microsoft's MAI-Thinking-1 and MAI family signal a new frontier-model competitor to OpenAI/Anthropic, shifting vendor calculus for Azure-heavy shops, but technical depth is thin in this single Latent Space recap.

LLM-as-judge unreliability in regulated-domain safety evals (finance explicitly named) is a concrete constraint for model risk teams building automated eval pipelines, but prior coverage and v2 status limit freshness.

Introduces 'weak-to-strong legibility' as a measurable property for multi-agent oversight—relevant to agentic pipeline design and AI safety governance, but no code/benchmark yet available to act on this week.

JudgmentBench offers DS/engineers a concrete benchmark comparing rubric vs. pairwise LLM-eval methodologies using 1,539 attorney annotations—directly actionable for teams designing LLM-as-judge eval pipelines.

DelibTrace formalizes a real failure mode in multi-agent deliberation—factual attrition and stance homogenization—giving practitioners a diagnostic lens for agent system design, though no code/repo is yet confirmed available.

BraveGuard introduces a self-evolving guard framework for computer-use agent safety—directly relevant to agentic deployment risk—but prior coverage was on a different topic (multilingual jailbreaks), making this substantively new; no code/repo yet limits hands-on utility.

Empirical finding that consistency training (RLHF-adjacent, scalable) can amplify misalignment across 108 model organisms is a real alignment risk signal for teams fine-tuning open-source models, but prior coverage and no code release limit freshness and utility.

METEORA's DPO-tuned rationale-driven RAG selection addresses interpretability and data-poisoning in sensitive domains—directly relevant to FS model-risk and compliance RAG deployments—but v5 repost with prior coverage limits freshness.

PAD offers an inference-time noise-injection defense for RAG privacy leakage — directly relevant to FS teams running RAG over sensitive data, but prior coverage and no public repo limit freshness and utility.
New method to predict Best-of-N inference scaling gains cheaply from validation stats before running end-to-end—useful for DS teams sizing inference budgets, but paper-only with no code yet.

EntSQL fills a real gap—benchmarking text-to-SQL against private business knowledge/internal metrics—directly relevant to FS teams building NL-to-data pipelines over proprietary schemas, though prior coverage and no code yet limit freshness and utility.

CoEval offers an open-source, contamination-free LLM ranking framework requiring no labeled data—directly useful for teams selecting models on proprietary tasks where public benchmarks are unreliable.

PROVE addresses a real bottleneck in agentic RL training—stateful MCP environments + verified rewards for multi-step tool use—with 20 servers and 343 tools practitioners can study, though prior coverage tempers freshness.
ALAR's dual-mode latent/explicit reasoning for multi-turn agents addresses real inference cost and efficiency gaps, but prior coverage and no released code limit immediate adoptability.

Chat-template 'ownership bias' worsens LLM calibration beyond instruction-tuning alone — directly relevant to model risk teams and any FS deployment where overconfident outputs drive decisions, but prior coverage limits freshness.

Rigorous decomposition of eval-awareness (recognition vs. behavioral response) gives benchmark designers a cleaner framework, but v2 update of prior-covered paper limits freshness and no code/benchmark is yet actionable.

AgentRedBench extends prior prompt-injection eval coverage with SaaS-integration-specific dynamic redteaming (Gmail/Salesforce/Jira), adding tool-response-trained guards relevant to FS agentic deployments, but prior coverage reduces freshness.

Calibration gap between LRM internal vs. expressed confidence is a real failure mode for agentic deployments, with direct model-risk implications for FS, but no code/benchmark yet and prior coverage reduces freshness.

Deep dive on xAI's Grok Imagine videogen pipeline (world models vs diffusion, 3-month build) is Tier-1 generative AI but hits only one signal dimension—tomorrow's trajectory—with no repo, API, or FS angle.
Geometric analysis of LLM-as-judge bias (inter-LLM consensus ≠ human alignment) is a real signal for eval pipelines, but prior coverage and no code/repo limit freshness and hands-on utility.

VeRO formalises agent-harness optimisation—agents iteratively editing and evaluating agent code—a Tier-1 agentic topic hitting tomorrow+practical signals, but prior coverage and v4 revision dampen freshness.

Reframes PEFT adapters as persistent per-user state atop shared foundation models—a conceptually useful framing for personalized agents—but v2 replace-cross with prior coverage and no code/benchmark limits immediate practitioner uptake.

Import AI newsletter digest covering AI oversight difficulty, protein-folding scaling laws, and extinction-risk pricing — big-picture signal for senior leaders but zero hands-on utility and no FS-specific anchors.

Tier-1 agentic/RAG topic (Direct Corpus Interaction vs. RAG pipelines) hits tomorrow and partially practical, but single-source AlphaSignal newsletter adaptation with no repo, code, or benchmark limits actionability.

RL-trained lightweight controller for adaptive test-time sampling is a real inference-cost technique (Tier 1, tomorrow signal), but prior coverage and no released code/model limit freshness and hands-on utility.

Hayek-inspired auction-based multi-agent self-orchestration is a novel decentralized coordination framing, but no code/benchmark is available yet and prior coverage reduces freshness; conceptually interesting, not actionable this week.
Live agent-eval benchmark built from real developer sessions addresses a genuine gap in agentic AI evals, but prior coverage and no FS angle limit freshness and impact scores.
SSR's self-play RL for coding agents is a meaningful training-paradigm signal but this is v3 of a prior-covered paper with no released code or benchmark, limiting immediate practitioner utility.
v4 update on LLM-as-Judge self-preference bias offers mitigation techniques practitioners building eval pipelines can apply, but prior coverage and incremental versioning limit freshness.
Interesting finding that unlearning one backdoor trigger generalizes to suppress others in LLMs, but no code/benchmark released and prior coverage exists; practical adoption path is thin this week.

Linear probes on mid-layer hidden states detect hallucination in 4-bit quantized 7-8B models—practically useful for eval pipelines, but prior coverage and no released code limit freshness and drop-in utility.
